summary: Gruber presents a practical developer tool he built: a Perl script that converts readable JavaScript source code into bookmarklet-ready URLs. The post explains the core problem — that bookmarklets require JavaScript to be crammed into URL-encoded single-line format, making development painful. His solution preserves the readable source code while generating the encoded bookmarklet as a comment at the top of the file and copying it to the clipboard. The script handles stripping comments, collapsing whitespace, and escaping special characters. It's designed to work as a BBEdit text filter but is editor-agnostic.
key_points: Bookmarklet development is irritating because JavaScript must be URL-encoded into a single line, making the code unreadable and hard to edit
The Perl script solves this by maintaining readable source code while generating the bookmarklet URL as a first-line comment, replaceable on each run
The script strips comments, collapses whitespace, removes newlines, and escapes special characters and unicode into a valid javascript: URL
The generated bookmarklet is also copied to the clipboard via pbcopy for immediate pasting into a browser's bookmark editor
Gruber deliberately keeps URL escaping minimal for readability, only escaping what's practically necessary rather than being maximally conservative
money_quotes: Developing or modifying bookmarklets can be irritating, to say the least, because of this requirement that the JavaScript code be in the form of a URL.
So the problem developing bookmarklets is this: You want to write and edit normal JavaScript code, but you need to publish hard-to-read URLs.
Personally, I prefer to keep the bookmarklet URL itself as readable as possible.
If it eats your source code, Undo is your friend.
key_insight: A simple build tool that converts readable JavaScript into bookmarklet URLs solves the fundamental tension between writing maintainable code and publishing browser-compatible bookmarklets.
